motifs.title: Isolated pair
motifs.desc: No kinship edge appears within 2 hops of either candidate. The marriage is arranged entirely outside the observable family network (or both families simply lacked prior register entries in Liaoning). Despite containing zero structural information, SEAL still scores these pairs at 0.85 -- confidence carried by the demographic feature channels (age, banner affiliation) rather than kin topology. This is the most common motif (503 training examples), reflecting that most Qing-dynasty Liaoning marriages cross unconnected lineages.

motifs.title: Shared mother (maternal half-siblings)
motifs.desc: A single woman appears as the mother of both the groom and the bride in the pre-marriage register. This creates a 3-node star: mother at the center connected to each candidate by r_ms. The candidates may be maternal half-siblings (different fathers) or the motif may reflect record-linkage quirks in the triennial registers. SEAL scores this pattern at 1.0 -- the shared-mother topology is the single strongest local predictor of marriage in the entire catalog, occurring 158 times. In historical context it likely captures inter-household matchmaking rooted in the mother's extended family network.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family of 4
motifs.desc: Both candidates share a father (r_fs) and a mother (r_ms), forming a complete nuclear household of 4 children: the pair plus 2 additional siblings. All sibling pairs are connected by bidirectional r_sib edges. The groom and bride occupy the two DRNL-1 (central) positions; the parents and extra siblings occupy DRNL-2 positions. Score 1.0 in 42 examples. Sociologically, this dense nuclear structure suggests close-kin alliance or that shared-household children were systematically matched by register administrators.

motifs.title: Compact nuclear family (2 siblings)
motifs.desc: The minimal full-sibling motif: both candidates share one father and one mother, and are each other's only recorded sibling in the 2-hop window. Four nodes total -- father, mother, groom, bride -- with r_fs and r_ms edges from both parents to both children, and a bidirectional r_sib link between the pair. Score 1.0 in 42 examples. This compact structure is the canonical nuclear household fingerprint in the CMGPD register: two children from the same home appear as the most structurally predictable marriage candidates.

motifs.title: Large sibling family (6 children)
motifs.desc: Both candidates share both parents within a household of 6 children (pair + 4 additional siblings). The 30 bidirectional r_sib edges form a fully-connected sibling clique. Score 1.0 in 29 examples. Large sibling sets in the Qing Liaoning registers indicate prolific households with strong social cohesion; SEAL identifies that children of such families were frequently matched together or with children of similarly structured allied households.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family of 3
motifs.desc: Pair plus one additional sibling, sharing both parents: a 5-node nuclear family. r_fs x3, r_ms x3, r_sib x6. Score 1.0 in 18 examples. The three-child household was the modal family size in Liaoning; this motif captures the most common nuclear-family alliance pattern where two of three siblings in one household form a marriage pair.

motifs.title: Shared mother (3 maternal children)
motifs.desc: A 4-node star rooted at a shared mother: r_ms x3 connecting her to the groom, bride, and one additional child. The pair's father is absent from the 2-hop window. Score 1.0 in 15 examples. This extended maternal-half-sibling pattern suggests that the mother's kin network or second-marriage household was the matchmaking nexus -- a distinct sociological signal from the full-sibling motifs.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family of 5
motifs.desc: 7 nodes: pair + 3 additional siblings sharing both parents. r_fs x5, r_ms x5, r_sib x20. Score 1.0 in 14 examples. Consistent with the larger-family motifs: as household size grows, the density of within-family sibling edges increases, giving SEAL a richer structural fingerprint and maximum predictive confidence.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family of 7
motifs.desc: 9 nodes: pair + 5 additional siblings, both parents present. 42 bidirectional r_sib edges form a clique of 7 siblings. Score 1.0 in 6 examples. This large family pattern is rarer in the Liaoning registers but carries maximum SEAL confidence, indicating that multi-child households produced highly predictable marriage networks.

motifs.title: Asymmetric 7-node family (more mothers than fathers)
motifs.desc: 7 nodes with r_fs x4 and r_ms x6: two paternal and three maternal lineage nodes are present, suggesting the subgraph spans a blended or remarriage household where the bride has more maternal-side relatives visible in the register. Score 1.0 in 6 examples. The asymmetry between paternal and maternal edges hints at differential household registration of male vs female lineage members in the Qing system.

motifs.title: Pure household co-residence (no kinship)
motifs.desc: Only r_hh edges: both candidates share a household node, and there are no blood-kinship edges (r_fs, r_ms, r_sib) within 2 hops. Score 0.077 -- this is the only motif with near-zero predictive power. At the LOCAL subgraph level, shared residence without recorded kinship is actually a negative signal for marriage. This seemingly paradoxical result is consistent with the macro-rule finding: r_hh carries 60% of HGT attention signal at the GLOBAL level, but at the local 2-hop level a shared household without kin structure may indicate servants, lodgers, or extended non-marriage co-residents rather than marriage candidates.

motifs.title: 9-person family with asymmetric parents
motifs.desc: 9 nodes: r_fs x7 and r_ms x6, giving a 7-sibling household where one parent has one fewer recorded child. Score 1.0 in 5 examples. The slight asymmetry (7 paternal vs 6 maternal records) likely reflects a remarriage, an illegitimate child recorded only under the father, or a transcription gap in the triennial register.

motifs.title: Extended family of 10 siblings
motifs.desc: 12 nodes: pair + 8 additional siblings sharing both parents. r_sib x90 (a fully connected 10-sibling clique). Score 1.0 in 5 examples. The largest symmetric family motif: very large households in Liaoning appear to have produced highly structured marriage networks captured perfectly by SEAL's subgraph topology.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family of 8
motifs.desc: 10 nodes, both parents, 8 children (pair + 6 siblings). r_fs x8, r_ms x8, r_sib x56. Score 1.0 in 5 examples. Another high-count sibling family. In historical Liaoning, households with 8+ children were unusual but real; their children formed dense social networks that SEAL captures as maximally marriage-predictive.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family + 2 shared households
motifs.desc: 8 nodes (6 persons + 2 household nodes): the pair and 2 siblings share both parents AND two household registrations. r_hh x16 links all family members to both households, indicating the family split across two registered living units -- common when a household branched. Score 1.0 in 4 examples. This is the simplest household-augmented motif: kin structure confirmed by multiple co-registration records.

motifs.title: 9-sibling family (slightly asymmetric parents)
motifs.desc: 10 nodes: r_fs x8, r_ms x7, r_sib x56. The bride has one fewer maternal edge recorded, suggesting one child may be from a different mother or a registration was missed. Score 1.0 in 4 examples. Structural near-equivalents to the symmetric 9-sibling motif but with subtle register asymmetry that SEAL resolves into a distinct motif hash.

motifs.title: Blended family (one father, two mothers)
motifs.desc: 5 nodes: r_fs x2 (one father to both candidates) but r_ms x4 (two distinct mothers, each connected to one or both candidates). Score 1.0 in 4 examples. This blended-family pattern may reflect a widower who remarried: the groom and bride are half-siblings through the father but have different mothers, a configuration that does appear in Qing registers where second marriages were common after spousal death.

motifs.title: 4-sibling family shared across 4 households
motifs.desc: 10 nodes (6 persons + 4 household nodes): a 4-child family (both parents) co-registered across four household entries. r_hh x28 shows dense multi-household membership typical of lineage sub-branches that maintained registrations in both an old household and a new one after a split. Score 1.0 in 3 examples.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family of 9
motifs.desc: 11 nodes: pair + 7 siblings, both parents. r_fs x9, r_ms x9, r_sib x72. Score 1.0 in 3 examples. One of the larger symmetric sibling-family motifs, reflecting a very prolific household with complete parental records.

motifs.title: 9-person family (more maternal links)
motifs.desc: 9 nodes: r_fs x6 and r_ms x8, r_sib x30. More maternal edges suggest a household where the mother's side was more fully recorded or the family included additional maternal half-siblings. Score 1.0 in 3 examples.

motifs.title: 8-person family (mixed parental records)
motifs.desc: 8 nodes: r_fs x5, r_ms x7, r_sib x20. The imbalance (7 maternal vs 5 paternal edges) points to an extended family where the mother has additional children from another union, or the father's full paternal links were only partially captured in the register. Score 1.0 in 3 examples.

motifs.title: Shared mother + shared household
motifs.desc: 4 nodes (3 persons + 1 household): groom and bride share a mother (r_ms x2) AND are co-registered in the same household (r_hh x2). The mother-household co-registration double-confirms the pair's residential and biological proximity before marriage. Score 1.0 in 3 examples.

motifs.title: Shared mother + two household registrations
motifs.desc: 5 nodes (3 persons + 2 household nodes): same shared-mother core as motif #2 but augmented with two household links (r_hh x4), indicating the family maintained entries in two successive household registers. Score 1.0 in 3 examples. The dual-household registration is consistent with a household that split between triennial surveys.

motifs.title: Extended family of 11 siblings
motifs.desc: 13 nodes: pair + 9 siblings, both parents. r_sib x110. Score 1.0 in 3 examples. This near-maximally large sibling clique represents one of the most structurally rich kinship patterns: every member is connected to every other member by both sibling links and parental links, giving SEAL an unambiguous topological signature.

motifs.title: Household-linked pair with weak kin signal
motifs.desc: 8 nodes: isolated pair (DRNL=0, no kin path) but connected via r_hh to a shared household, with 1 r_fs and 1 r_ms edge present. Score 0.903 -- near-maximum but slightly reduced because the kin connection is thin (one paternal, one maternal link) and the primary connection is through the household node. This motif occupies a structurally intermediate position between the pure-household negative motif (#11) and the full-kin motifs.

motifs.title: Mega-clan (52 nodes)
motifs.desc: The largest motif in the catalog: 52 persons and 1 household node, with r_fs x36, r_ms x50, r_sib x532, r_hh x84. This represents an extended multi-generational clan spanning multiple sibling groups that all appear within 2 hops of the marriage pair. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. Historically, such dense kin networks correspond to prominent Qing banner families with meticulously maintained multi-generational registers. SEAL's maximum confidence reflects that marriage within dense mega-clans follows highly predictable structural paths.

motifs.title: 5-sibling family + shared household
motifs.desc: 8 nodes (7 persons + 1 household): r_fs x5, r_ms x5, r_sib x20, r_hh x10. The pair plus 3 siblings share both parents and are all co-registered in a single household. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The household confirmation reinforces the kin signal: co-residence + co-parentage in the same register entry is near-definitive.

motifs.title: Extended family of 12 siblings
motifs.desc: 14 nodes: r_fs x12, r_ms x11, r_sib x132. Slightly asymmetric parental records (12 vs 11 links). Score 1.0 in 2 examples. One of the top sibling-count motifs, with 132 sibling edges forming a near-complete clique among 12 children of the household.

motifs.title: Full-sibling family of 7 (symmetric)
motifs.desc: 9 nodes: r_fs x6, r_ms x6, r_sib x30 -- perfectly balanced paternal and maternal records for a 7-sibling household. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The symmetric parental records make this a structurally cleaner motif than the asymmetric 7-sibling variants.

motifs.title: Multi-household clan (44 nodes, 13 households)
motifs.desc: The second-largest motif: 31 persons + 13 household nodes. r_fs x29, r_ms x27, r_sib x412, r_hh x294. This sprawling clan spans 13 household registrations, consistent with a major lineage branch that maintained entries across many triennial survey periods. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The extreme household density suggests an elite Qing banner family with property holdings registered in multiple administrative units.

motifs.title: Paternal-dominant lineage (father-only side visible)
motifs.desc: 12 nodes: r_fs x11, r_ms x2, r_sib x74. The radical imbalance (11 paternal vs 2 maternal edges) suggests the subgraph captures a patrilineal lineage cluster where maternal records are sparse -- a common pattern in Qing registers where women's origins were less thoroughly documented. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. Historically, this reflects the patrilineal emphasis of the banner household registration system.

motifs.title: Small family + single shared household
motifs.desc: 8 nodes (7 persons + 1 household): r_fs x3, r_ms x3, r_sib x2, r_hh x12. A compact 3-sibling nuclear family (1 additional sibling beyond the pair) with dense household co-registration (12 r_hh edges). Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The high r_hh count relative to family size suggests all family members appeared in the household roster across multiple register cycles.

motifs.title: Large family + 4 shared households
motifs.desc: 18 nodes (14 persons + 4 household nodes): r_fs x12, r_ms x12, r_sib x68, r_hh x48. A symmetric 12-sibling family (or 10-sibling with asymmetric overlapping) spread across 4 household registrations. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The combination of maximum kin density and multi-household spread identifies a prosperous lineage segment.

motifs.title: Asymmetric 8-node family
motifs.desc: 8 nodes: r_fs x6, r_ms x5, r_sib x30. One fewer maternal edge creates a minor asymmetry in a 7-sibling household. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. Structurally distinct from the symmetric 7-sibling motif (#29) due to the missing maternal link -- possibly a half-sibling among the group.

motifs.title: Nuclear family of 3 (slightly asymmetric)
motifs.desc: 5 nodes: r_fs x3, r_ms x2, r_sib x6. A 3-child nuclear family where one child has only a paternal (not maternal) link -- possibly a half-sibling from the father's side. Score 1.0 in 2 examples.

motifs.title: 6-household extended clan
motifs.desc: 20 nodes (14 persons + 6 household nodes): r_fs x12, r_ms x12, r_sib x80, r_hh x72. A large clan where 12 siblings are spread across 6 household registrations. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The 6-household spread with symmetric parental records suggests a wealthy lineage that actively partitioned itself across multiple administrative households while maintaining kin records.

motifs.title: Compact nuclear family + single household
motifs.desc: 5 nodes (4 persons + 1 household): r_fs x2, r_ms x2, r_sib x2, r_hh x4. The minimal household-augmented motif: groom and bride plus both parents all co-registered in one household. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. This is the simplest confirmation of pre-marriage household co-residence alongside shared parentage.

motifs.title: 9-household sprawling clan
motifs.desc: 31 nodes (22 persons + 9 household nodes): r_fs x17, r_ms x13, r_sib x158, r_hh x156. A sprawling extended family with asymmetric parental records (17 paternal vs 13 maternal) spread across 9 households. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The combination of patrilineal bias and extreme household spread is characteristic of major banner sub-clans.

motifs.title: 5-household extended family
motifs.desc: 24 nodes (19 persons + 5 household nodes): r_fs x17, r_ms x14, r_sib x212, r_hh x44. A large sibling group spread across 5 households, with slight paternal bias. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. 212 sibling edges indicate ~14-15 siblings in a complex network, making this one of the structurally richest household-kin combinations in the catalog.

motifs.title: Mother-heavy 3-sibling family
motifs.desc: 6 nodes: r_fs x3, r_ms x5, r_sib x6. More maternal edges (5) than paternal (3) in a 3-child household. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The maternal emphasis is the mirror of the paternal-dominant motifs: here the mother's side is more thoroughly documented, possibly reflecting a matrifocal household or a well-recorded female lineage in the Liaoning banner register.

motifs.title: 8-sibling family (father-heavy)
motifs.desc: 10 nodes: r_fs x8, r_ms x6, r_sib x56. Eight paternal vs six maternal edges in an 8-sibling group. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. Consistent with the general pattern of paternal-register completeness in the CMGPD: the father's name was recorded more reliably than the mother's across multiple registers.

motifs.title: 6-household extended clan (symmetric parents)
motifs.desc: 22 nodes (16 persons + 6 household nodes): r_fs x14, r_ms x14, r_sib x84, r_hh x72. Perfectly symmetric parental records across a 6-household network. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The symmetry distinguishes this clan from the asymmetric 6-household motif (#36): both parents are equally well-documented across all branches.

motifs.title: Complex family + single household (asymmetric)
motifs.desc: 10 nodes (9 persons + 1 household): r_fs x6, r_ms x8, r_sib x30, r_hh x12. A 7-sibling subgraph with more maternal edges, all co-registered in one household. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The household ties all family members together even as the maternal lineage records dominate.

motifs.title: Compact 4-sibling family (dense)
motifs.desc: 6 nodes: r_fs x4, r_ms x3, r_sib x12. A 4-child household with near-symmetric parental records (one maternal edge missing). Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The 12 sibling edges form a fully-connected clique among the 4 children.

motifs.title: Paternal-only 8-sibling family
motifs.desc: 10 nodes: r_fs x8, r_ms x1, r_sib x56. One maternal edge only -- nearly all kin connections pass through the father. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. This extreme paternal bias likely indicates a family where the mother was poorly documented (died early, not present in the register, or her ID was not linked), but the father's lineage was consistently recorded across the 2-hop window.

motifs.title: 4-household sprawling clan (mother-heavy)
motifs.desc: 27 nodes (23 persons + 4 household nodes): r_fs x18, r_ms x21, r_sib x212, r_hh x82. More maternal edges than paternal, spread across 4 households, with 212 sibling edges indicating ~15 siblings. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The maternal dominance is unusual at this scale and may reflect a matrilineal household branch or a family with particularly well-documented female ancestors.

motifs.title: 4-sibling family + household (variant A)
motifs.desc: 7 nodes (6 persons + 1 household): r_fs x4, r_ms x4, r_sib x12, r_hh x6. A 4-sibling nuclear family with symmetric parents and modest household co-registration (6 r_hh links). Score 1.0 in 2 examples. Closely related to variant B (#48) but differs in the number of household membership records.

motifs.title: 4-sibling family + household (variant B)
motifs.desc: 7 nodes (6 persons + 1 household): r_fs x4, r_ms x4, r_sib x12, r_hh x8. Structurally identical to variant A (#47) except for 8 vs 6 r_hh edges, indicating all 4 siblings plus both parents appeared in the household roster across more register cycles. Score 1.0 in 2 examples.

motifs.title: Nuclear pair + shared household
motifs.desc: 6 nodes (5 persons + 1 household): r_fs x2, r_ms x2, r_sib x2, r_hh x8. The pair shares both parents (minimal full-sibling structure) and is heavily co-registered in one household (8 r_hh edges across multiple surveys). Score 1.0 in 2 examples. The high r_hh count relative to family size indicates persistent co-residence documentation across many triennial cycles.

motifs.title: 4-household medium clan
motifs.desc: 18 nodes (14 persons + 4 household nodes): r_fs x12, r_ms x11, r_sib x80, r_hh x40. A 10-sibling family spread across 4 household entries, with slight paternal edge excess. Score 1.0 in 2 examples. Sits between the compact household-augmented motifs and the mega-clan outliers: a mid-sized extended family with complete kin and multi-household records.

score_explanation: The SEAL score is the output of the trained SEAL classifier applied to this 2-hop enclosing subgraph pattern. It estimates the probability that this local pre-marriage kinship structure is associated with a real marriage event in the Qing Liaoning registers. Score 1.0 means the model assigns near-certainty to any marriage pair that shows this local kin topology. Score 0.85 (isolated pair) reflects high demographic confidence (age + banner match) even without visible kin structure. Score 0.90 (partial household link) reflects moderate structural evidence. Score 0.077 (pure household co-residence) is the only negative-signal motif: shared residence alone, without kinship edges, is a weak local predictor because co-residents include servants, lodgers, and non-marriage-eligible household members. Note that r_hh carries 60% of HGT global attention -- the household signal operates at the network-wide level, not in local 2-hop subgraphs.
